text: Name the models of the atom shown in the figure (Model A and Model B).
mark_scheme: Model A: plum pudding (allow Thomson model); Model B: Bohr (allow nuclear, planetary, or Rutherford-Bohr model).

text: Compare model A (Plum Pudding) with the model of the atom used today. Use the figure provided.
mark_scheme: Level 2 (3-4 marks): Scientifically relevant features identified and compared clearly. Indicative content: Similarities: both contain electrons, both are neutral overall. Differences: Model A has no nucleus/protons/neutrons; Model A is a ball of positive charge vs today's central positive nucleus; Model A has random electron distribution vs today's shells/energy levels; Model A has mass spread throughout vs today's concentrated mass in center; Model A has no empty space vs today's mostly empty space.

text: Define the term ‘isotopes’. Refer to subatomic particles in your answer.
mark_scheme: Atoms with the same number of protons (allow atoms of the same element/atomic number) with different numbers of neutrons.

text: Which particle has no electrical charge? [Options: Electron, Neutron, Proton]
Which particles have the same relative mass? [Options: Electron and neutron, Electron and proton, Neutron and proton]
The formula of a compound is N2O. How many of each type of atom are in one molecule of N2O?
An atom of element Z contains 3 electrons, 4 neutrons, and 3 protons. Give the name of element Z using the periodic table.
Complete the figure to show the position of the particles in an atom of element Z (3 electrons, 4 neutrons, 3 protons).
mark_scheme: 3 protons and 4 neutrons in the nucleus; 3 electrons arranged in shells (2, 1 configuration).

text: Describe the plum pudding model of the atom.
mark_scheme: A ball of positive charge (do not accept protons/nucleus) with (negative) electrons embedded.

text: Write these three particles in order of their discovery: electrons, neutrons, protons.
mark_scheme: Earliest: Electrons; Middle: Protons; Latest: Neutrons.

text: The atomic number of tennessine (Ts) is 117. Predict the number of outer shell electrons and give a reason.
mark_scheme: Number: 7; Reason: Tennessine is in Group 7 (allow: same as group number or it is a halogen).

text: Suggest one reason why tennessine was not accepted as a new element by other scientists until 2015, despite being identified in 2010.
mark_scheme: Time needed for peer review (allow the idea that other scientists had to check the results).

text: Calculate the relative atomic mass (Ar) of element R using isotopes: Mass 6 (7.6%) and Mass 7 (92.4%). Give answer to 1 decimal place.
Draw lines to label the parts of the atom (Neutron, Proton, etc.).
mark_scheme: Correct matching of subatomic particles to their locations (nucleus vs shells).

text: An atom of element Y has an atomic number of 9 and a mass number of 19. Give the number of electrons and neutrons.
Calculate the relative atomic mass (Ar) of element Z using isotopes: Mass 39 (93.3%) and Mass 41 (6.7%). Give answer to 3 significant figures.
Suggest the identity of element Z (Ar = 39.1).
Isotopes of the same element have different mass numbers because the isotopes have different numbers of __________.
Which type of substance is hydrogen? [Options: Element, Compound, Mixture]
Using Figure 1, which diagram (A, B, C, D) represents a mixture of compounds?
Using Figure 1, which diagram (A, B, C, D) represents a mixture of elements?
Sand can be separated from a mixture of sand and water by __________.
A mixture of four liquids (A: 97°C, B: 138°C, C: 78°C, D: 118°C) was distilled. Which liquid is collected first?
Suggest what would happen to the temperature of the water as it flows through the condenser.
Describe how to obtain sodium chloride crystals from sodium chloride solution by crystallisation.
mark_scheme: Heat the solution until crystallisation point is reached (or start to form/reduce volume); leave the solution to cool/crystallise.
